summary: COMPLETE AUDIT. Manuscript: 90,892 prose words, 7,395 sentences, 3,651 prose lines across 18 chapters. REDUNDANCY: 29 findings. The manuscript's dominant redundancy pattern is role-as-metaphor accumulation — Joel's public-defender identity is proved 38+ times (15 pure re-provings), Paul's insurance-adjuster 14 times, Cliff's sales persona 20+ times, Tommy's coaching voice 30+ times, and Nate's podcast-brain 22+ times. Physical re-descriptions are moderate: Tommy's crew cut and hands motif are repeated verbatim in Ch 2 (adjacent to Ch 1); Cliff's 'pressed chinos' outfit and 'overdressed for a lake house' observation are re-described four times across Chs 1/7/12/14. The dock/stone-path spatial inventory (eighty yards, pine and birch, dock thirty feet) is taught four times from scratch across Chs 5/10/12/14. The refrigerator cycling motif (58 occurrences across 17 chapters) is the most overused image; scotch/Lagavulin (39 occurrences) is second. Em-dash density is 1.21% (44/3651 prose lines) — well under threshold. Sentence-opener 'He' is 6.44% manuscript-wide (476/7395) but spikes to 12.0% in Ch 17 and 11.1% in Ch 11. All hedge patterns are below 1/800 words. One near-verbatim 11-word cross-chapter match found (Tommy in Martin's chair, Chs 16-17). CONSISTENCY: 3 findings — voicemail timestamp contradiction (8:17 vs 8:47 PM), safe cash amount contradiction (just over $2000 vs $1960), fire date contradiction (August 10 vs August 11, 2002).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 43: 'crew cut graying at the temples, hands that were always gripping something. A beer, a shoulder, the back of his own neck.' Ch 2 line 564: 'his crew cut flattened on one side from the pillow.' Ch 2 line 632: 'the way his hands gripped the back of his own neck and then dropped and then gripped again.' Ch 2 line 716: 'Tommy's hands were always in motion, holding a beer, gripping a shoulder, gesturing at a point.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Tommy's crew cut (verbatim) and hands-gripping motif with the same item list (beer, shoulder, back of neck) are established in Ch 1 line 43 and re-described three times within Ch 2 (lines 564, 632, 716). The hands-gripping passage at line 716 is near-verbatim from line 43. This is adjacent-chapter redundancy.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut 'crew cut' at line 564 or replace with a detail not yet established. At line 632, vary the hands motif — remove 'the back of his own neck.' At line 716, cut or sharply abbreviate the itemized list ('holding a beer, gripping a shoulder'). Tommy's hands in pockets as disrupted norm works without re-listing the usual items.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 2 no longer contains the phrase 'crew cut' or a list of items Tommy's hands grip that overlaps with Ch 1 line 43's list.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 43: 'Six-two and still built like something functional.' Ch 2 line 564: 'coaching shorts.' Ch 5 line 1633: 'Apple Valley football' / 'Eastview.' Ch 8 line 2446: 'Tommy Renaldi was forty-seven, a high school football coach and PE teacher, married with three kids and a public life built in a suburb where Friday nights still meant something.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 8 delivers a full name-plus-role-plus-family re-introduction of Tommy (age, occupation, family status, community role) as a single dossier-style block, as if the reader has not spent seven chapters with him. Tommy's coaching role was established in Chs 1, 2, 3, and confirmed in Ch 5's interview.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or sharply reduce the expository summary at line 2446. Cliff's calculation can reference what Tommy stands to lose without re-stating his full CV. Replace 'a high school football coach and PE teacher, married with three kids' with a compressed reference that assumes the reader knows Tommy.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 8 no longer contains a passage stating Tommy's age, occupation, and family status together as a single expository block.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 43: 'Six-two and still built like something functional.' Ch 12 line 3476: 'He outweighed Cliff by forty pounds, and every pound had been built on purpose, in weight rooms and on practice fields.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Tommy's build is re-described in Ch 12 using near-synonymous phrasing to Ch 1 ('built like something functional' vs. 'built on purpose, in weight rooms and on practice fields'). The weight-room/practice-field origin repeats what Ch 1 established.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the size contrast with Cliff (the forty-pound difference is new tactical information) but cut 'in weight rooms and on practice fields.' The differential alone conveys the threat.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 12 retains the weight differential but no longer explains where Tommy's mass was built.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 45: 'like he'd confused the lake house with a client dinner.' Ch 7 line 2041: 'Cliff came in from the hallway showered and looking ready for a client meeting, coffee mug in hand, the only man in the house who seemed to believe Monday still existed.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Cliff's overdressed-for-the-lake-house observation is established in Ch 1 line 45 ('confused the lake house with a client dinner') and repeated in Ch 7 line 2041 with near-synonymous phrasing ('looking ready for a client meeting'). The joke is identical.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the 'client meeting' comparison. Keep Cliff showered and brisk but let Doug's POV notice something different about Cliff's entry rather than re-telling the Ch 1 joke.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 7 no longer compares Cliff's appearance to a client meeting, client dinner, or any professional appointment.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 45: 'confused the lake house with a client dinner.' Ch 7 line 2041: 'looking ready for a client meeting.' Ch 12 line 3370: 'Cliff stood there freshly shaved and too put together for a lake house, like he'd stepped out to show a shoreline lot instead of sit on a dead friend's deck.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Cliff's overdressed-at-the-lake-house observation appears a third time in Ch 12. The real-estate-listing metaphor is new phrasing for the same observation made in Chs 1 and 7.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the simile 'like he'd stepped out to show a shoreline lot' and the judgment 'too put together for a lake house.' Tommy's POV here is menacing — let him notice something dangerous about Cliff rather than re-registering grooming.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 12 no longer explicitly states Cliff is too put-together for a lake house or compares his appearance to a professional showing.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 45: 'pressed chinos and a collared polo.' Ch 14 line 4620: 'Pressed chinos, navy polo, and the leather loafers he wore indoors because he was the one person in the group who gave a shit about shoes.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Cliff's outfit is re-described in Ch 14 with 'pressed chinos' verbatim from Ch 1 and 'navy polo' echoing 'collared polo.' This is the fourth re-description of Cliff's grooming pattern (Chs 1, 7, 12, 14).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the outfit line. Doug's POV strength here is room dynamics, not Cliff's clothing. If physical detail is needed, use something new.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 14 no longer contains 'pressed chinos' or a clothing inventory for Cliff.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 4 line 986: 'In twenty years as a public defender in Hennepin County, he'd never once considered that side of the room.' Ch 5 line 1358: 'twenty years in arraignment rooms and interview rooms had given him a feel for what secrecy did to air.' Ch 5 line 1381: 'A public defender who knew exactly how much silence was worth.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Joel's 'twenty years as a public defender' is stated in Ch 4 (line 986) and restated in near-identical framing in Ch 5 (line 1358) — adjacent chapters. Ch 5 also adds a second 'public defender' label at line 1381.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 5 line 1358, cut 'twenty years.' Joel can observe secrecy in the room without restating tenure. At line 1381, Mallick's shorthand 'the lawyer' suffices — cut the standalone public-defender label.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 5 no longer contains a 'twenty years' reference to Joel's career or a standalone sentence identifying him as a public defender.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 19: 'a flannel that cost a hundred and fifty dollars and looked like it cost twelve, which was Martin's whole approach to possessions.' Ch 11 line 3177: 'three polo shirts, the expensive kind Martin bought to look casual, as if a sixty-dollar polo was casual.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Martin's expensive-but-casual clothing trait is established in Ch 1 and re-described in Ch 11 with the same structural observation: price + ironic note about casualness.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 11, keep the polo shirts as inventory but cut the editorial gloss about buying expensive things to look casual. The polos can exist without re-teaching Martin's consumption philosophy.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 11 no longer contains an editorial comment about Martin buying expensive clothing to appear casual.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 5 lines 1597-1601: 'seventy or eighty yards away through the pines and birch... The stone path to the dock.' Ch 10 line 3124: 'the stone path that led through the pines and birches to the dock, eighty yards of pale stone in the dark, ending at the wooden dock that ran thirty feet into the lake.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The dock/stone-path spatial inventory (stone path, pines and birch, ~80 yards, dock) is established in Ch 5 and re-taught from scratch in Ch 10.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the spatial inventory. Mallick can see the dock through the window without re-specifying path material, trees, and distance. Enter through what is different (darkness) rather than restating the blueprint.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 10 no longer specifies the distance to the dock or itemizes the stone path through pines and birches.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 5: stone path, pines and birch, ~80 yards. Ch 10: eighty yards, dock thirty feet. Ch 12 line 3654: 'It lay thirty feet out... The stone path to it ran through enough pine and birch to make anybody private.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Third re-inventory of the dock/stone-path. 'Thirty feet' repeats Ch 10's dock length; 'stone path... pine and birch' repeats Chs 5 and 10.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the stone-path sentence. Keep 'boards with a slight give' (new tactile detail) but drop the path-through-trees blueprint. Tommy knows this path.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 12 no longer describes the stone path's route through pine and birch or restates the dock's length.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 5: stone path, pines, ~80 yards. Ch 10: eighty yards, thirty feet. Ch 12: thirty feet, stone path through pine and birch. Ch 14 lines 4646-4654: 'Eighty yards through pine and birch... extending thirty feet into the lake.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Fourth re-inventory. 'Eighty yards through pine and birch' and 'thirty feet' repeat exact numbers and tree species from Chs 5, 10, and 12.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the eighty-yard distance and thirty-foot dock length. Keep the unique sensory texture (flagstones sunk from freeze-thaw, moonlight cut by canopy, turpentine-and-sugar smell).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 14 no longer specifies eighty yards or thirty feet in the dock path description.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 4 lines 986, 1010, 1012, 1076, 1112, 1120, 1210, 1236, 1274, 1354, 1358: Joel's public-defender/lawyer/courtroom/cross-examination identity is proved 11 times within a single chapter. E.g., 'In twenty years as a public defender' (986); 'I'm not being a lawyer' / 'You're always being a lawyer' (1010-12); 'watching opposing counsel work a jury' (1112); 'You're the attorney' (1120); 'like a cross-examination' (1236); 'old courtroom habit of cataloging the room' (1354).
redundancy_findings.problem: Joel's lawyer identity is re-proved 11 times in Ch 4 alone — far beyond the 3-instance threshold. Multiple formulations say the same thing: Joel processes the world through legal frameworks. By this chapter, the reader has already met Joel's 'public defender' identity in Ch 1 line 79.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Joel's lawyer-labeling in Ch 4 to no more than 3 instances. Keep the strongest (the arraignment reversal at 986, the Paul dialogue at 1010-12, and one courtroom-metaphor observation). Cut the rest — especially 'old courtroom habit' (1354) and 'like a cross-examination' (1236) which repeat the Ch 1 line 79 formulation.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 4 contains no more than 3 passages that label Joel's behavior through his legal profession.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 18 lines 7180, 7246, 7260, 7266: 'already in courtroom mode' (7180); 'Joel's lawyer face stayed on' (7246); 'as if he was trying to keep himself from cross-examining the walls' (7260); 'satisfy the lawyer in him' (7266). By Ch 18, Joel's lawyer identity has been proved 35+ times previously.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 18 re-proves Joel's lawyer baseline 4 times in 90 lines after the trait has been established beyond saturation. The cross-examination metaphor at line 7260 is the fifth use of this specific formulation (also at Ch 1 line 79, Ch 4 line 1236, Ch 9 line 2655).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one lawyer reference in Ch 18 (the strongest is 'courtroom mode' at 7180 as a reversal setup). Cut the other three. Joel's behavior in Ch 18 should speak for itself without label.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 18 contains no more than 1 passage labeling Joel's behavior through his legal profession.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 5 lines 1379, 1391-1395, 1467, 1479, 1507: 'the insurance man' (1379); 'Claims adjuster... Mutual of Omaha, but the Minneapolis office' (1393); 'insurance precision' (1467); 'like claims work, isn't it?' (1479); 'sat like he was at a claims desk' (1507). Five insurance-adjuster labels for Paul in one chapter, after Ch 2 line 753 and Ch 4 lines 1092-1100 already proved this baseline.
redundancy_findings.problem: Paul's insurance-adjuster identity is re-proved 5 times in Ch 5 on top of the 3-4 times it was proved in Chs 2 and 4. The specific employer (Mutual of Omaha) at line 1393 is new information; the rest are pure baseline re-proving. 'Insurance precision' and 'claims desk' and 'claims work' all say the same thing.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the Mutual of Omaha detail (1393, new info) and one behavioral observation. Cut 'insurance precision' (1467), 'like claims work' (1479), and 'claims desk' (1507) — these are three versions of the same metaphor in one chapter.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 5 contains Paul's employer name and no more than 1 additional insurance-metaphor label.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 12 lines 3333, 3396, 3466, 3478, 3494: 'Cliff turns everything into a breakfast conversation. Or a real estate seminar' (3333); 'Cliff sold things. That was the center of him' (3396); 'The salesman face was still there' (3466); 'stopped hearing the sales pitch' (3478); 'Salesmen hated silence the way linemen hated open space' (3494). Five sales-identity labels in 160 lines.
redundancy_findings.problem: Cliff's sales/smooth/real-estate baseline is re-proved 5 times in 160 lines of Ch 12, after already being established 10+ times in Chs 1-11. The pattern ('Cliff sold things' / 'salesman face' / 'sales pitch' / 'salesmen') is the same observation in four different wordings.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep 'Cliff sold things. That was the center of him' (3396, strongest) as Tommy's core assessment. Cut the other four instances. Tommy's menace in this chapter should come from action, not repeated labeling of Cliff's persona.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 12 contains no more than 2 passages labeling Cliff through his sales identity.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 5 lines 1379, 1591, 1651, 1874, 1880, 1974: Mallick's reading glasses are mentioned 6 times in Ch 5: 'reading glasses pushed to the end of her nose' (1379); 'reading glasses pushed up on her forehead' (1591); 'over her glasses' (1651); 'reading glasses were on the beaded chain' (1874); 'put her reading glasses on' (1880); 'took off her reading glasses and let them hang on the beaded chain' (1974).
redundancy_findings.problem: Mallick's reading-glasses-on-beaded-chain prop is introduced and then cycled through 6 on/off/push/hang actions in a single chapter. This establishes the glasses as a re-introduction tag rather than a meaningful character detail. The same on/off cycle repeats in Chs 7 and 10.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce glasses mentions in Ch 5 to 2: the introduction (1379) and one active use. Cut the remaining 4 on/off business actions. The glasses can exist without being an every-scene prop.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 5 mentions Mallick's reading glasses no more than twice.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 14 lines 4660, 4672, 4676: 'Contraindicated, his pharmaceutical vocabulary said' (4660); 'Doug's pharmaceutical training gave clinical names for' (4672); 'not the pharmaceutical sales voice' (4676). Three pharmaceutical-identity labels in 16 lines, after Ch 7 line 1995 and Ch 13 line 3947 already established this baseline.
redundancy_findings.problem: Doug's pharmaceutical background is re-proved 3 times in 16 lines of Ch 14, after 2 prior establishments. The same word ('pharmaceutical') is used as a character label three times in rapid succession during the dock discovery scene.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one pharmaceutical reference in the dock discovery (strongest is 'Contraindicated' at 4660, which shows the vocabulary in action). Cut the other two explicit 'pharmaceutical' labels.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 14's dock discovery scene contains no more than 1 passage labeling Doug through his pharmaceutical background.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 167: 'Doug agreed to things.' Ch 4 line 1354: 'Doug sunk down in his hoodie, saying almost nothing and noticing almost everything.' Ch 9 line 2593: 'the quiet surveillance Doug had perfected over twenty-five years of being the one nobody watched.' Ch 14 line 4616: 'Watching rooms was what he'd always done.' Ch 14 line 4632: 'Doug was the one you told things to when you needed someone to nod.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Doug's quiet-observer baseline is stated in essentially the same language 4 times (Chs 4, 9, 14 twice). Line 4616 ('watching rooms was what he'd always done') and 4632 ('the one you told things to when you needed someone to nod') are the fourth and fifth versions of Ch 4's 'saying almost nothing and noticing almost everything.'
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 14, cut the explicit character-summary sentences at lines 4616 and 4632. Doug's observation can be shown through what he notices in the scene rather than declared as his defining trait for the fourth time.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 14 no longer contains a standalone sentence declaring that Doug watches rooms or is the quiet one.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 11 line 3213: 'Martin was organized even in death.' Ch 11 line 3245: 'Martin had organized even his absence.' Same sentence, same chapter, 32 lines apart.
redundancy_findings.problem: Martin's controlling/organizing baseline is stated twice in near-identical phrasing within 32 lines of Ch 11. 'Organized even in death' and 'organized even his absence' are the same observation.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut one of the two. Keep whichever better serves the surrounding paragraph's emotional arc (3245 is stronger because it connects to Paul's anger about the will).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 11 contains only one version of the 'Martin organized even his [death/absence]' observation.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Manuscript-wide: 'refrigerator' or 'fridge' appears 58 times across 17 of 18 chapters. Ch 18 has the highest concentration with 8 instances. The cycling/grinding/arthritic description repeats in nearly identical form across chapters (Ch 4 line 1352: 'kicked on, loud and rhythmic'; Ch 5 line 1387: 'kicked into its grinding cycle'; Ch 9 line 2525: 'cycling hum'; Ch 17 line 6047: 'arthritic cycles'; Ch 18: 'arthritic cycle').
redundancy_findings.problem: The refrigerator appears 58 times manuscript-wide (target: 8-10 for 20+ occurrences). Ch 18 alone has 8 instances. The 'cycling/grinding/arthritic' motif has become a tic rather than an image — the reader is re-oriented to the same ambient sound in nearly every chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 18, reduce refrigerator mentions from 8 to 2. Keep the strongest instance (the one anchoring Mallick's final kitchen-table scene). Cut the cycling/arthritic description — by Ch 18 the reader does not need to be told how the fridge sounds.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 18 contains no more than 2 references to the refrigerator and does not describe its cycling sound.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Manuscript-wide: 'refrigerator' or 'fridge' appears 58 times. Ch 1 has 7 instances. The fridge-as-ambient-sound motif starts strong in Ch 1 and never lets up.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 1 introduces the refrigerator 7 times, establishing it as a too-prominent environmental element from the start. Target is 8-10 instances manuscript-wide, so Ch 1 alone uses most of the budget.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce refrigerator mentions in Ch 1 from 7 to 2-3. Keep the strongest atmospheric uses. The fridge can exist without being foregrounded in every kitchen scene.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 1 contains no more than 3 references to the refrigerator.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Manuscript-wide: refrigerator/fridge appears 58 times. Ch 7 has 7 instances. Combined with Ch 1 (7) and Ch 9 (6), the early-to-mid manuscript is saturated.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 7 contributes 7 refrigerator mentions to the 58 total. The cycling-fridge sound appears again in this chapter as ambient texture identical to Chs 1, 4, and 5.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch 7 refrigerator mentions from 7 to 1-2. The kitchen can be set without the fridge cycling on cue.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 7 contains no more than 2 references to the refrigerator.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Manuscript-wide: 'scotch' appears 25 times, 'Lagavulin' 14 times (39 combined, with some overlap on shared lines). Ch 1 has 9 scotch/Lagavulin mentions — the highest chapter concentration.
redundancy_findings.problem: Martin's scotch is mentioned 9 times in Ch 1 alone, nearly half the target budget for the entire manuscript (8-10 for 20+ occurrences). The Lagavulin brand name and scotch references saturate the opening chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce scotch/Lagavulin references in Ch 1 from 9 to 3-4. Establish the brand once, the drinking habit once, and use 1-2 in-scene references. The draft-night atmosphere can carry without every scene reminding the reader of the scotch.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 1 contains no more than 4 references to scotch or Lagavulin.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Manuscript-wide: scotch/Lagavulin appears 39 times. Ch 14 has 6 instances. By Ch 14, the scotch has been referenced in Chs 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 11 already.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 14 mentions scotch or Lagavulin 6 times after the image has appeared 25+ times previously. The bottle-on-the-counter detail at line 4620 ('the bottle had been on the counter since Thursday night') recaps information the reader has had since Ch 7.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch 14 scotch references from 6 to 2. Keep the strongest (Cliff pouring himself two fingers is plot-relevant). Cut the editorial recap about the bottle being on the counter since Thursday.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 14 contains no more than 2 references to scotch or Lagavulin.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Manuscript-wide: scotch/Lagavulin appears 39 times. Ch 2 has 7 instances, second highest after Ch 1.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 2 mentions scotch or Lagavulin 7 times on top of Ch 1's 9 — giving the reader 16 scotch references in the first two chapters. The adjacent-chapter density is excessive.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch 2 scotch references from 7 to 2-3. The discovery and CPR scenes carry their own weight without repeated scotch-smell anchoring.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 2 contains no more than 3 references to scotch or Lagavulin.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 16 line 5976: 'Tommy sat outside in Martin's chair, broad shoulders rounded inward, a beer warming on the armrest he wasn't touching.' Ch 17 line 5999: 'Tommy was in the Adirondack chair closest to the railing, Martin's chair whether anybody called it that or not, with a beer balanced on the armrest he was not touching.'
redundancy_findings.problem: An 11-content-word near-verbatim passage spans consecutive chapters: 'Martin's chair... a beer [warming/balanced] on the armrest he was[n't] [not] touching.' The image is essentially identical — Tommy sitting in Martin's chair with an untouched beer on the armrest.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 17, rephrase Tommy's position to avoid echoing Ch 16's specific construction. The beer and the chair can appear without the identical 'on the armrest he was not touching' formula. Alternatively, cut the untouched-beer-on-armrest detail from one chapter.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 17's description of Tommy in the chair no longer shares 10+ consecutive content words with Ch 16 line 5976.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 17: 78 sentences beginning with 'He' out of 652 total sentences = 12.0%. Manuscript-wide 'He' rate: 476/7395 = 6.44%. Ch 17 is the worst offender, nearly double the manuscript average.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 17 (Paul POV) opens 12.0% of its sentences with 'He' — 78 of 652 sentences. This is the highest rate of any chapter and nearly double the manuscript-wide average. The pattern creates rhythmic monotony in Paul's extended retrospective chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Vary at least 30 of the 78 'He' openers in Ch 17 by using clause inversions, participial phrases, or object-first constructions. Target bringing the chapter below 7% (max 45 'He' openers).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 17 contains fewer than 46 sentences beginning with 'He' (under 7% of chapter sentences).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 11: 41 sentences beginning with 'He' out of 371 total sentences = 11.1%. Manuscript-wide 'He' rate: 6.44%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 11 (Paul POV) opens 11.1% of its sentences with 'He' — the second-worst chapter rate. Combined with Ch 17 (also Paul POV at 12.0%), Paul's chapters are the primary driver of 'He'-opener monotony.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Vary at least 15 of the 41 'He' openers in Ch 11. Target bringing the chapter below 7% (max 25 'He' openers).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 11 contains fewer than 26 sentences beginning with 'He'.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 10: 45 sentences beginning with 'She' out of 346 total sentences = 13.0%. Manuscript-wide 'She' rate: 195/7395 = 2.64%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 10 (Mallick POV) opens 13.0% of its sentences with 'She' — far above the manuscript-wide average. This is the most extreme single-pronoun-opener spike in the manuscript.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Vary at least 20 of the 45 'She' openers in Ch 10. Target bringing the chapter below 7% (max 24 'She' openers). Mallick's analytical voice can use more passive constructions, clause inversions, or evidence-first sentence structures.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 10 contains fewer than 25 sentences beginning with 'She'.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 6 line 1836 (Nate POV): Nate recalls the voicemail timestamp as '8:17 PM, three minutes forty-one seconds.' Ch 10 line 2910 (Mallick POV): Mallick's evidence report says the voicemail was 'received at 8:47 PM Friday.'
consistency_findings.problem: The same voicemail (Nate's long draft-pick rant left on Martin's phone Friday evening) is given two different timestamps: 8:17 PM in Nate's recollection (Ch 6) and 8:47 PM in Mallick's evidence summary (Ch 10). This is a 30-minute discrepancy on a plot-critical piece of evidence.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Pick one timestamp and make both chapters consistent. If 8:47 PM is canonical (matching the continuity sheet), change Ch 6 line 1836 from '8:17 PM' to '8:47 PM.' If 8:17 PM is intended, change Ch 10 line 2910.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The voicemail timestamp is identical in Ch 6 and Ch 10.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 8 line 2380 (Cliff POV): 'The stack was twenties and fifties, just over two thousand dollars.' Ch 11 line 3201 (Paul POV): 'Nineteen hundred and sixty dollars.'
consistency_findings.problem: Cliff and Paul each count the same clipped stack of bills in Martin's safe without removing the clip. Cliff estimates 'just over two thousand dollars' but Paul counts '$1,960.' Since $1,960 is just UNDER two thousand, Cliff's 'just over' description is contradictory. No one removes cash between the two scenes.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change Cliff's estimate at Ch 8 line 2380 from 'just over two thousand dollars' to 'close to two thousand dollars' or 'just under two thousand.' Alternatively, change Paul's count to $2,040 to match 'just over.'
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Cliff's estimate and Paul's exact count are compatible — both describe the same amount as either just over or just under two thousand.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 8 line 2414 (RT document text as Cliff reads it): 'Saturday night, August 10, 2002.' August 10, 2002 was indeed a Saturday. Ch 16 line 5750 (Joel paraphrasing the same document): 'The fire was the night of August 11, 2002.' The document header says August 17, described as 'six days after the fire' — but Aug 17 minus Aug 10 = 7 days, not 6; Aug 17 minus Aug 11 = 6 days.
consistency_findings.problem: The Ray Toomey fire date is given as August 10, 2002 in the original document text (Ch 8) and as August 11, 2002 in Joel's paraphrase (Ch 16). The 'six days after' math at line 5750 supports August 11, but the document's own text says August 10 (which was a Saturday, matching 'Saturday night'). One of these dates is wrong.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: If August 10 is canonical (it was a Saturday, matching the document's 'Saturday night'), change Ch 16 line 5750 from 'August 11' to 'August 10' and adjust 'six days' to 'seven days' (or 'a week after'). If August 11 is intended, change Ch 8 line 2414 and note that August 11, 2002 was a Sunday, not a Saturday.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The fire date is identical in Ch 8 and Ch 16, the day-of-week matches the calendar, and the 'days after' arithmetic is correct relative to the August 17 document date.
